papers.title: Deep Learning Predicts Dissimilar DNA-DNA Binding and Engineers Hyperconnected Networks.
papers.abstract: Common frameworks in molecular bioengineering and synthetic biology focus on orthogonality, viewing weak or non-specific interactions as problems to avoid. This constrains the usable sequence space, limits scalability, and neglects scenarios where synthetic systems must operate within natural backgrounds of high sequence diversity. Harnessing the full space is difficult because models are lacking that can accurately and quickly predict non-orthogonal interactions and be validated against ground truth data. Here we develop BINND - Binding and Interaction Neural Network for DNA - using DNA-DNA i
papers.summary: The authors developed BINND, a deep learning model that predicts non-orthogonal DNA-DNA binding interactions, enabling the design of hyperconnected networks rather than avoiding weak or cross-reactive binding. This approach expands the usable sequence space for synthetic biology and molecular bioengineering by harnessing interactions previously considered problematic.

papers.title: A new protein panel for the diagnosis of HFpEF: combining machine-learning and liquid-chromatography mass-spectrometry proteomics.
papers.abstract: The diagnosis of heart failure with a preserved left ventricular ejection fraction (HFpEF) remains challenging. Plasma concentrations of NT-proBNP can rule out HFpEF with good sensitivity, but only moderate specificity. Therefore, there is a high unmet need for better diagnostic biomarkers with improved specificity.
papers.summary: This study identifies a new panel of protein biomarkers that can improve the diagnosis of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), a condition that is currently difficult to diagnose accurately. By combining machine learning with mass spectrometry proteomics, the researchers found proteins that offer better specificity than the standard NT-proBNP test, potentially reducing misdiagnosis.

papers.title: Mind the gap: An embedding guide to safely travel in sequence space.
papers.abstract: We present a hybrid approach combining a protein language model (pLM) with Monte Carlo (MC) sampling for generating enzyme mutants free of mutations deleterious for structural preservation. Given the amino acid sequence of the original enzyme and a set of residues for which the local environment should be conserved, i.e., the catalytic site, our approach generates mutants that differ vastly in the overall sequence while retaining the geometry of the conserved region, thereby representing promising candidates for further experimental screening. Unlike end-to-end deep-learning approaches, whose
papers.summary: This paper presents a hybrid method that combines a protein language model with Monte Carlo sampling to generate enzyme mutants that preserve the structure of key functional regions (like catalytic sites) while allowing large changes elsewhere in the sequence. This approach offers a more targeted and interpretable alternative to end-to-end deep learning for designing novel enzyme variants for experimental screening.

papers.title: Association and predictive value of preoperative high-sensitivity C-reactive protein for postoperative atrial fibrillation after video-assisted thoracoscopic lobectomy: a cohort study using the INSPIRE database.
papers.abstract: Postoperative atrial fibrillation (POAF) is a common and serious complication following video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS), which affects patients' medical outcomes and quality of life. The predictive value of high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) as an inflammatory marker for POAF in non-cardiac surgeries remains unclear. This study aims to investigate the association and predictive capability of preoperative hs-CRP levels with postoperative new-onset atrial fibrillation.
papers.summary: This study found that higher preoperative levels of high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) are associated with an increased risk of developing atrial fibrillation after video-assisted thoracoscopic lung surgery. Using data from the INSPIRE database, the researchers suggest that hs-CRP may serve as a predictive marker for this common postoperative complication. The findings could help identify high-risk patients before surgery.

papers.title: Integrative profiling of senescence-associated cells and genes identifies UGT1A7 as a prognostic and therapeutic target in Lung squamous cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Lung Squamous Cell Carcinoma (LUSC), a common subtype of non-small cell lung cancer, primarily occurs in elderly individuals and is closely linked to senescence-related biological processes. Meanwhile, senescence reshapes the tumor microenvironment by promoting immune suppression and angiogenesis, and its underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Here, we identified three senescence-related molecular subtypes of LUSC, each with distinct clinical outcomes, genomic alterations, and immune features. Among them, Cluster 1 was associated with poor prognosis, increased genomic instability, and an immuno
papers.summary: This study identifies three distinct senescence-related molecular subtypes in lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC) with different clinical outcomes and immune features. The gene UGT1A7 is highlighted as a potential prognostic marker and therapeutic target, particularly in a poor-prognosis subtype characterized by genomic instability and immune suppression.

papers.title: Predicting 3-year mortality after myocardial infarction in a structured care program: A comparative analysis of Cox regression and machine learning models.
papers.abstract: Accurate risk stratification after myocardial infarction (MI) remains essential for optimizing long-term management. The advantage of machine learning (ML) over conventional statistical models in real-world clinical settings remains uncertain.
papers.summary: This study compared Cox regression and machine learning models for predicting 3-year mortality after myocardial infarction in a structured care program, finding that machine learning did not significantly outperform the traditional Cox model. The results suggest that conventional statistical methods remain effective for risk stratification in real-world clinical settings.

papers.title: Phylogenetic analysis and machine learning identify signatures of selection and predict deleterious mutations in common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris L.).
papers.abstract: Mutations are a continuous source of new alleles and genetic diversity in populations. Domestication and selection influence the accumulation of alleles occurring across a range of deleteriousness. Evidence suggests that mildly deleterious mutations (DelMut) can be purged out of breeding populations, increasing favorable allele accumulation.
papers.summary: This study combines phylogenetic analysis and machine learning to identify signatures of selection and predict deleterious mutations in common bean. The findings suggest that domestication and selection can purge mildly deleterious mutations, thereby increasing the accumulation of favorable alleles in breeding populations.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: This paper presents an open dataset of 32 million three-dimensional structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences, generated using an automated computational workflow. This resource enables high-throughput virtual screening and structure-based analysis of small peptides without experimental determination.

papers.title: AI/ML-Enabled Multi-Omics Integration of Host Genetics, Immunity, and the Gut Microbiome in Crohn's Disease: From Diagnosis to Theranostics.
papers.abstract: Crohn's disease is a long-term inflammatory disorder arising from the interaction of genetic risk factors, immune system dysfunction, and alterations in gut microbiota. Variability in clinical phenotypes and lack of biomarker specificity hinder the efficiency of current traditional diagnostic and treatment approaches. This review aims to assess how AI- and ML-driven multi-omics offer comprehensive insights into pathogenicity, thereby enhancing diagnostic techniques and personalized therapeutic approaches in CD. Current studies employ integration of multi-omics like genomics, proteomics, transc
papers.summary: This review examines how AI and machine learning can integrate multiple types of biological data (genomics, proteomics, transcriptomics, and gut microbiome) to better understand Crohn's disease. The authors argue that such multi-omics approaches could improve diagnosis and enable personalized treatment by revealing the complex interactions between genetics, immunity, and gut bacteria that drive the disease.

papers.title: Evolution of CTCF binding sites in the human genome.
papers.abstract: The CCCTC-binding factor (CTCF) is a master regulator of topologically associating domains (TADs), which shape 3D genome architecture and gene regulation. While TAD evolution across species has been extensively studied, the evolution of CTCF binding sites (CBSs) in primates remains under-explored. Here, we employed a deep learning model to predict genome-wide CBSs in five primate species, including human, chimpanzee, gorilla, orangutan, and macaque. We found that approximately half of human CBSs are conserved across all five species. These primate-conserved CBSs cluster near TAD boundaries, ex
papers.summary: This study uses a deep learning model to predict CTCF binding sites (CBSs) across five primate species, finding that about half of human CBSs are conserved across all species. These conserved CBSs cluster near TAD boundaries, highlighting their importance in 3D genome architecture and gene regulation.

papers.title: Smiles-based bioactivity prediction through molecular encoder selection and data augmentation.
papers.abstract: Quantitative prediction of inhibitor potency can accelerate early-stage drug discovery. Recently, data-driven approaches have gained widespread interest in drug discovery, as evidenced by a growing number of benchmarking challenges and open competitions. In this context, we developed a machine learning-based methodology that can find the most effective way of predicting IC50 values against ASK1 from SMILES, for "Jump AI(.py) 2025: 3rd AI Drug Discovery Competition", hosted by the Korea Pharmaceutical and Bio-Pharma Manufacturers Association (KPBMA) on the Dacon platform. Applying our methodolo
papers.summary: This study presents a machine learning pipeline that selects optimal molecular encoders and applies data augmentation to predict IC50 values of inhibitors against ASK1 from SMILES strings. The method was developed for the Jump AI(.py) 2025 drug discovery competition, demonstrating a practical approach to improving bioactivity prediction accuracy.

papers.title: A self-attention-based deep learning model for identifying key genes in insect pupal metamorphosis.
papers.abstract: Metamorphosis is the major innovation in the evolution of insect, playing an important role in environmental adaptation and biodiversity formation. However, it has been a challenge to identify the key genes in insect pupal metamorphosis. In this study, we constructed a library of word embeddings based on Word2vec for 293 insect protein sequences from 14 orders. A gene network classification model (GNCM) based on deep learning (DL) and self-attention mechanisms (SAM) was designed to identify key genes by calculating their importance (weights) in the metamorphosis of insect pupae. Empirical stud
papers.summary: This study developed a deep learning model using self-attention mechanisms and Word2vec embeddings to identify key genes involved in insect pupal metamorphosis. The model was trained on protein sequences from 293 insect species across 14 orders and can calculate gene importance weights to pinpoint critical metamorphosis-related genes.

papers.title: A population-based retrospective machine learning study of COVID-19 severity using integrated clinical and viral genomic data in Jiangsu Province, China.
papers.abstract: As coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) has transitioned into an endemic phase characterized by sustained transmission and widespread hybrid immunity, understanding region-specific determinants of severe disease remains important for real-world risk stratification and public health planning.
papers.summary: This study used machine learning to analyze clinical and viral genomic data from COVID-19 patients in Jiangsu Province, China, identifying key factors associated with severe disease. The findings help improve risk stratification for severe outcomes in the endemic phase, supporting public health planning.

papers.title: Harnessing Large-Scale Multi-Omics Data for Risk Prediction and Deep Phenotyping of Valvular Heart Diseases in the General Population.
papers.abstract: The risk profile of valvular heart disease (VHD) and its underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to develop and validate a multi-omics-based risk prediction model, and to elucidate potential biological mechanisms. Using data from the UK Biobank, Cox proportional hazards and machine learning models (XGBoost and LightGBM) were evaluated for predicting VHD and its subtypes (aortic valve stenosis, AVS; aortic valve regurgitation, AVR; mitral valve regurgitation, MVR). Cox models based on key clinical factors showed the best predictive performance (C-index of 0.75-0.81), wh
papers.summary: This study uses UK Biobank data to build risk prediction models for valvular heart diseases (VHD) by integrating clinical, genomic, and other multi-omics data. The best performance came from Cox models using key clinical factors, achieving good predictive accuracy for VHD subtypes. The work also explores biological mechanisms underlying VHD risk.

papers.title: Accurate prediction of asparagine deamidation in biologics using advanced machine learning models.
papers.abstract: The spontaneous deamidation of asparagine residues remains a major obstacle to the stability and efficacy of protein therapeutics. Currently available models in the literature for predicting deamidation liabilities can suffer from limited generalizability, likely due to biases such as sequence similarity within datasets. In this study, we built machine learning models using protein language models (PLMs, e.g. ESM2) and graph neural networks (GNNs), trained on a comprehensive dataset of 591 asparagine sites from over 105 protein molecules. To address the critical issue of data leakage, we imple
papers.summary: Researchers developed machine learning models using protein language models and graph neural networks to predict asparagine deamidation in protein therapeutics, training on a dataset of 591 asparagine sites from over 105 proteins. The models address data leakage issues and improve generalizability compared to existing tools, offering a more reliable method for identifying deamidation liabilities in biologics.

papers.title: ProMeta: a meta-learning framework for robust disease diagnosis and prediction from plasma proteomics.
papers.abstract: The plasma proteome offers a dynamic window of human health, capturing the real-time intersections between genetics and physiology. However, the application of deep learning to proteomics is currently hindered by a reliance on large-scale labeled datasets, rendering standard models ineffective for rare or novel diseases where patient samples are inherently scarce.
papers.summary: ProMeta is a new meta-learning framework designed to improve disease diagnosis and prediction from plasma proteomics data, even when only small labeled datasets are available. This approach addresses a key limitation of deep learning in proteomics, enabling effective analysis of rare or novel diseases where patient samples are scarce.

papers.title: A novel transformer model of protein domains for viral taxonomy classification.
papers.abstract: Viruses with carefully curated taxonomic assignments (such as those in the ICTV taxonomy) still represent only a small fraction of viruses identified through sequencing data from virome or microbiome projects. It is therefore critical to develop methods that can assign viruses at multiple taxonomic ranks, so that a virus deemed novel at a given rank may still be placed into a higher-level taxon. Sequence-similarity-based approaches can classify viruses that share substantial genomic similarity with known viruses (e.g. those belonging to the same species or genus); however, their performance dr
papers.summary: This paper introduces a transformer-based model that uses protein domain information to classify viruses across multiple taxonomic ranks, addressing the challenge of classifying novel viruses that lack close genomic similarity to known viruses. The approach outperforms traditional sequence-similarity methods, particularly for higher-level taxonomic assignments where genomic similarity is low.

papers.title: USP-ddG: a unified structural paradigm with data efficacy and mixture-of-experts for predicting mutational effects on protein-protein interactions.
papers.abstract: Accurately estimating changes in binding free energy (ΔΔG) is critical for understanding protein-protein interactions (PPIs) and guiding rational protein design. Recent deep learning methods have achieved notable progress by pre-training on large-scale structural data. While some approaches explore structural flexibility through energy-based sampling or generative modeling, these strategies typically involve substantial computational cost and overlook data efficacy in terms of training data organization.
papers.summary: USP-ddG is a new deep learning method that predicts how mutations affect protein-protein binding strength without expensive simulations. It uses a unified structural representation and a mixture-of-experts approach to efficiently learn from training data, achieving accurate predictions at lower computational cost.

papers.title: NPBIP: predicting binding preferences of uncharacterized nucleic-acid-binding proteins.
papers.abstract: Nucleic-acid-binding proteins (NBPs) are crucial regulators of gene expression, recognizing specific RNA or DNA binding sites. While high-throughput experiments have generated vast amounts of binding data, a significant challenge remains in predicting binding affinities of a novel query NBP to any nucleic-acid sequence. Current computational methods often require prior experimental data for the query NBP or are limited to predictions over predefined short RNA or DNA sequences.
papers.summary: NPBIP is a new computational tool that predicts how any nucleic-acid-binding protein (NBP) will bind to RNA or DNA sequences, even if no prior experimental data exists for that protein. It overcomes limitations of existing methods that require pre-existing binding data or can only handle short sequences. This could accelerate research on uncharacterized NBPs involved in gene regulation.

papers.title: PU-GRAIL: residue-level graph learning for identifying protective bacterial antigens under positive-unlabeled supervision.
papers.abstract: The identification of protective antigens is fundamentally constrained by sparse annotations and pervasive label uncertainty in reverse vaccinology. Protective antigen-antibody interactions are mediated by a limited subset of surface-accessible residues that form spatially coherent epitopes. This motivates modeling antigenicity at the residue level, where 3D structure provides critical context for functional immune recognition. Moreover, antigen datasets are inherently positive unlabeled: unannotated proteins may contain hidden positives, making reliable negatives difficult to obtain.
papers.summary: This paper introduces PU-GRAIL, a graph neural network that predicts protective bacterial antigens by analyzing protein surface residues in 3D structure, using a positive-unlabeled learning approach to handle the pervasive label uncertainty in vaccine target discovery. The method models antigen-antibody interactions at the residue level, capturing spatially coherent epitopes, and outperforms existing tools by effectively learning from datasets where unannotated proteins may contain hidden protective antigens.

papers.title: OrgNet+: towards robust protein stability prediction with convolutional neural networks.
papers.abstract: Predicting the effect of single-point mutations on protein stability is a central problem in molecular biology and protein engineering. Recent structure-based deep learning methods, particularly 3D convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs), have achieved strong predictive performance by leveraging high-resolution protein structures. However, proteins exist as heterogeneous conformational ensembles rather than single static structures, and the impact of conformational flexibility on structure-based ΔΔG predictors remains poorly characterized. Consequently, current models may yield unstable or eve
papers.summary: This paper introduces OrgNet+, a 3D convolutional neural network that improves protein stability prediction by accounting for the conformational flexibility of proteins, rather than relying on single static structures. The authors show that incorporating ensemble-based representations leads to more robust and reliable predictions of mutation effects on protein stability.

papers.title: seq2ribo: structure-aware integration of machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences.
papers.abstract: Ribosome dynamics are vital in the process of protein expression. Current methods rely on ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq), RNA-seq profiles, and full genomic context. This restricts their use in de novo sequence design, like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines. Simulation-only approaches like the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) oversimplify translation by focusing solely on codon elongation times.
papers.summary: seq2ribo is a new computational tool that combines machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles directly from RNA sequences, without needing experimental data like Ribo-seq or full genomic context. This enables its use in de novo sequence design, such as for mRNA vaccines, overcoming limitations of current methods that rely on experimental profiles or oversimplified simulations.

papers.title: Agentomics: an agentic system that autonomously develops novel state-of-the-art solutions for biomedical machine learning tasks.
papers.abstract: Extracting knowledge from biomedical data is crucial for advancing our understanding of biological systems and developing novel therapeutics. The quantity, quality, and resolution of biomedical data constantly evolves, requiring the automation of biomedical machine learning (ML). Existing Automated ML tools lack flexibility, while large language models (LLMs) struggle to consistently deliver reproducible machine learning codebases, and existing LLM Agent-powered solutions lag behind human-engineered ML models.
papers.summary: The paper introduces Agentomics, an AI agent system that autonomously develops state-of-the-art machine learning solutions for biomedical tasks. It overcomes limitations of existing automated ML tools and LLM-based approaches by generating reproducible, high-performing codebases that can match or exceed human-engineered models.

papers.title: Human gut flagellome profiling using FlaPro reveals TLR5-related phenotype-specific alterations in IBD.
papers.abstract: Flagellin, the structural protein of bacterial flagella, activates the innate immune receptor Toll-like receptor 5 (TLR5). However, the ability of different flagellins to bind and stimulate TLR5 varies widely, suggesting that the composition of an individual's flagellin repertoire, defined as flagellome, may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation. Here, we developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline for quantification and functional annotation of human gut flagellomes. Functional categories in FlaPro are derived from a machine learning model trained on experimentally characteri
papers.summary: The study introduces FlaPro, a computational pipeline that quantifies and functionally annotates the flagellin repertoire (flagellome) of the human gut microbiome. Using this tool, the authors identify TLR5-related phenotype-specific alterations in the flagellome of individuals with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), suggesting that the composition of flagellins may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation.

papers.title: Growth factor thermostability dataset derived from experimental and computational approaches to accelerate cultured meat development.
papers.abstract: This project compiled experimentally measured and computationally predicted thermostability data for growth factors (GFs) commonly used in cultured meat and seafood (CM) production. Data were generated on recombinant GFs using thermal shift assays and combined with publicly available data using a structured literature curation workflow developed using a Large Language Model (LLM) with manual validation. In addition, in silico protein stability calculations were generated using FoldX. Data such as GF identity, species origin, recombinant host, variant information, method of measurement, and exp
papers.summary: This paper presents a dataset of thermostability measurements for growth factors used in cultured meat production, combining experimental thermal shift assays with computational predictions from FoldX and literature curation using a Large Language Model. The resource includes information on growth factor identity, species origin, and measurement methods, which could help optimize growth factor stability for cultured meat manufacturing.

papers.title: Growth-rate analysis of clathrin assembly: inferring endocytic kinetics in living cells and tissues.
papers.abstract: Measuring protein kinetics in living cells remains challenging due to the complexity of intracellular environments and the limitations of live-cell imaging. Clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME) provides a model system in which protein assembly dynamics encode functional outcomes, yet conventional approaches rely on reconstructing full trajectories of individual events, making them sensitive to tracking errors and difficult to extend to dense or in vivo systems. Here, we review growth rate-based analysis of clathrin assembly as an alternative framework for quantifying endocytic kinetics. By extr
papers.summary: This review presents growth-rate analysis as an alternative method for quantifying clathrin-mediated endocytosis kinetics, which avoids the tracking errors and limitations of conventional trajectory-based approaches in dense or in vivo systems. The method focuses on measuring assembly growth rates from live-cell imaging data, offering a more robust framework for studying protein dynamics in complex environments.

papers.title: AI-Powered Discovery of Rosmarinic Acid as a Novel Ferroptosis Inhibitor for Ulcerative Colitis via Targeting the ALOX15-VDAC1 Axis.
papers.abstract: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease with limited mechanism-based therapies, and ferroptosis-an iron-dependent lipid peroxidation-driven cell death-has emerged as a critical pathogenic event. Using a machine learning model that combines molecular fingerprints and transfer learning representations to screen for ALOX15 inhibitors, we identified rosmarinic acid (RA) from natural product libraries. RA exhibited high-affinity binding to ALOX15 and maintained stable complex conformation in molecular dynamics simulations. In dextran sulfate sodium-induced colitis mice and l
papers.summary: This study used a machine learning model combining molecular fingerprints and transfer learning to screen for inhibitors of ALOX15, identifying rosmarinic acid (RA) as a novel ferroptosis inhibitor. RA showed high-affinity binding to ALOX15 and effectively treated ulcerative colitis in mice by targeting the ALOX15-VDAC1 axis, offering a potential new therapy for this chronic inflammatory disease.

papers.title: Machine learning-based prediction of difficult laryngoscopy in infants with Pierre Robin sequence using quantitative 3D computed tomography parameters.
papers.abstract: Infants with Pierre Robin sequence (PRS) frequently present with difficult laryngoscopic exposure due to mandibular hypoplasia, glossoptosis, and upper airway narrowing. Quantitative three-dimensional computed tomography (3D-CT) enables objective characterization of airway anatomy and may improve preoperative airway assessment. This study aimed to identify key 3D-CT parameters associated with difficult laryngoscopic exposure in infants with PRS and to develop and externally validate machine learning-based predictive models.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict difficult laryngoscopy in infants with Pierre Robin sequence by analyzing quantitative 3D CT parameters of the airway. The models identify key anatomical features associated with poor laryngoscopic exposure, potentially improving preoperative risk assessment.

papers.title: Discovery of hub genes linking oxidative stress to type 2 diabetic sarcopenia using single-cell sequencing and machine learning.
papers.abstract: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) and sarcopenia demonstrate a significant comorbidity, particularly in the elderly, yet the molecular mechanisms linking them, especially through oxidative stress, remain incompletely understood. This study aimed to identify oxidative stress-related hub genes involved in T2DM-associated sarcopenia (T2DS) by integrating single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and bulk RNA-seq data with machine learning. We analyzed scRNA-seq datasets (GSE244515, GSE268953) to characterize cellular heterogeneity and bulk RNA-seq datasets (GSE202295, GSE226151) for differential expre
papers.summary: This study identifies key genes linking oxidative stress to sarcopenia in type 2 diabetes by combining single-cell and bulk RNA sequencing with machine learning. The findings highlight potential molecular targets for understanding and treating muscle wasting in diabetic patients.

papers.title: SST subseasonal-to-seasonal forecasting: A heterogeneous three-path fusion model with wavelet decomposition.
papers.abstract: El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO), the Earth's dominant mode of interannual climate variability, is closely linked to sea surface temperature (SST) variability in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific. Accurate subseasonal-to-seasonal SST forecasting in this region supports ocean monitoring and environmental assessment. We propose WDFormer (Wave Decomposition Former), a daily SST forecasting model built on a "decomposition, multi-path processing, and adaptive fusion" paradigm. The model uses 365 daily SST observations from local 4×4 grids around representative ENSO monitoring stations t
papers.summary: This paper presents WDFormer, a deep learning model that improves subseasonal-to-seasonal sea surface temperature (SST) forecasting by decomposing SST data with wavelet transforms and fusing multiple processing pathways. The model uses daily SST observations from local grid regions around ENSO monitoring stations to enhance prediction accuracy. This work is relevant for climate monitoring and environmental assessment.
